story.legal_language: The appeals raised questions regarding the interpretation of Section 142(a) of the Negotiable Instruments Act, 1881, specifically whether a Power of Attorney holder is competent to: (a) sign and file a complaint under Section 138 NI Act satisfying the eligibility criterion of 'payee or holder in due course'; (b) verify the complaint on oath under Section 200 CrPC; and (c) depose as a witness. A reference to a three-judge bench was necessitated by apparent conflict between MMTC Ltd. vs Medchl Chemicals (2002) 1 SCC 234 and Janki Vashdeo Bhojwani vs Indusind Bank (2005) 2 SCC 217.
story.plain_language: Two cheque-dishonour cases were heard together. In the first (A.C. Narayanan), a Power of Attorney holder (Mrs. Doreen Shaikh) filed complaints on behalf of six payees against the accused for dishonoured cheques; the accused challenged whether a PoA holder could file/verify the complaint. In the second (G. Kamalakar), a company's PoA holder filed a complaint for dishonoured cheques; the accused was acquitted by the trial court but convicted on appeal by the High Court, which conviction was challenged before the Supreme Court. Both cases raised the common legal question: can a PoA holder file and depose in a Section 138 NI Act complaint?
story.verification_note: All facts verified against judgment paragraphs 1-5, 7, 17-18

obiter_dicta.observation: Various situations such as inability due to sickness, old age, death or staying abroad of the payee or holder in due course justify permitting a PoA holder or legal representative to file a complaint and/or continue pending criminal complaints on behalf of the payee or holder in due course.
obiter_dicta.future_significance: May be used to extend the ratio to cases of incapacity; could support arguments for legal heirs or representatives filing/continuing Section 138 complaints beyond the specific grounds listed

obiter_dicta.observation: Under the Statute, a stranger to the transaction such as a legal heir can carry forward a pending criminal complaint or initiate criminal action if the original complainant dies [citing Ashwin Nanubhai Vyas].
obiter_dicta.future_significance: Affirms and extends the right of legal heirs to continue Section 138 proceedings; useful in estate/succession contexts

obiter_dicta.observation: Where the payee is a proprietary concern, the complaint can be filed (i) by the proprietor describing himself as sole proprietor of the payee; (ii) the proprietary concern describing itself as a sole proprietary concern represented by its sole proprietor; and (iii) the proprietor or the proprietary concern represented by the attorney holder under a power of attorney executed by the sole proprietor.
obiter_dicta.future_significance: Provides a template for proprietary concerns on how to structure Section 138 complaints; useful guidance for practitioners

ratio_decidendi.label: PoA holder may file, appear and depose in Section 138 complaint if filed in name of/on behalf of payee
ratio_decidendi.proposition: Filing of a complaint petition under Section 138 of the NI Act through a Power of Attorney holder is perfectly legal and competent. The PoA holder cannot file the complaint in his own name as if he were the complainant, but can initiate criminal proceedings on behalf of the principal/payee. The eligibility criterion of Section 142(a) is satisfied when the complaint is filed in the name of and on behalf of the payee or holder in due course.

ratio_decidendi.label: PoA holder may depose only if he has personal knowledge of the transaction
ratio_decidendi.proposition: The Power of Attorney holder can depose and verify on oath before the Court to prove the contents of the complaint, provided the PoA holder has witnessed the transaction as an agent of the payee/holder in due course or possesses due knowledge regarding the said transactions. A PoA holder without personal knowledge of the transaction cannot be examined as a witness.

ratio_decidendi.label: Complaint must explicitly assert PoA holder's knowledge of the transaction
ratio_decidendi.proposition: It is required that the complainant make a specific assertion as to the knowledge of the Power of Attorney holder in the said transaction explicitly in the complaint. A PoA holder who has no knowledge of the transactions cannot be examined as a witness.

ratio_decidendi.label: Magistrate's examination under Section 200 CrPC is discretionary in Section 138 cases in light of Section 145 NI Act
ratio_decidendi.proposition: In light of Section 145 of the NI Act, the Magistrate may rely upon verification in the form of an affidavit filed by the complainant in support of the complaint under Section 138. The Magistrate is neither mandatorily obliged to call upon the complainant to remain present before the Court, nor to examine the complainant or witness upon oath for the purpose of deciding whether or not to issue process.

ratio_decidendi.label: Sub-delegation of PoA requires explicit authorisation in the instrument
ratio_decidendi.proposition: The authority to sub-delegate the functions of a general power of attorney must be explicitly mentioned in the general power of attorney. Without such explicit clause, sub-delegation is inconsistent with the general power of attorney and invalid in law. However, the general power of attorney itself can be cancelled and a fresh one given to another person.

precedent_registry.case_name: M.M.T.C. Ltd. and Anr. vs. Medchl Chemicals and Pharma (P) Ltd. and Anr.
precedent_registry.why_cited: First precedent on whether PoA/authorised agent can file Section 138 complaint; created apparent conflict with Janki Vashdeo Bhojwani
precedent_registry.what_it_held: Complaint under Section 138 NI Act can be made by the payee or holder in due course; the eligibility criterion of Section 142 is satisfied if the complaint is filed in the name of and on behalf of the payee company; complaints filed by Manager of Regional Office on behalf of company were held maintainable

precedent_registry.case_name: Janki Vashdeo Bhojwani and Anr. vs. Indusind Bank Ltd. and Ors.
precedent_registry.why_cited: Created apparent conflict with MMTC; raised question whether PoA holder can depose in Section 138 proceedings; necessitated the reference
precedent_registry.what_it_held: Power of attorney holder cannot depose for acts done by principal or for facts within principal's exclusive personal knowledge; PoA holder can only appear as witness in respect of facts within his personal knowledge; word 'acts' in Order 3 Rule 2 CPC does not include 'deposing' in place of principal

precedent_registry.case_name: Vishwa Mitter vs. O.P. Poddar and Ors. (also referred to as Vishwa Mitter of M/s Vijay Bharat Cigarette Stores vs. O.P. Poddar)
precedent_registry.why_cited: Supports the general principle that criminal law can be set in motion by anyone; used to support proposition that Section 200 CrPC does not create embargo on PoA holder being complainant
precedent_registry.what_it_held: Anyone can set criminal law in motion by filing a complaint of facts constituting an offence; no court can decline cognizance solely because complainant was not competent; if special statute prescribes eligibility, that criterion must be satisfied

Dishonour of cheque for insufficiency, etc., of funds in the account.- Where any cheque drawn by a person on an account maintained by him with a banker for payment of any amount of money to another person from out of that account for the discharge, in whole or in part, of any debt or other liability, is returned by the bank unpaid, either because of the amount of money standing to the credit of that account is insufficient to honour the cheque or that it exceeds the amount arranged to be paid from that account by an agreement made with that bank, such person shall be deemed to have committed an offence and shall, without prejudice to any other provisions of this Act, be punished with imprisonment for a term which may be extended to two years, or with fine which may extend to twice the amount of the cheque, or with both: [Proviso clauses (a)(b)(c) and Explanation as reproduced in judgment]

stress_test.step_1_statutory_basis: Section 142's non-obstante clause overrides CrPC provisions; however, the clause specifically refers to CrPC, not to general principles of agency law or the Janki Vashdeo Bhojwani personal knowledge rule which was derived from CPC context; the argument conflates different legal domains
stress_test.step_2_structural_validity: Gate 3 analysis: Section 142 is a standalone eligibility/cognizance provision; Section 145 is a separate evidence provision; the non-obstante clause in Section 142 overrides CrPC but does not expressly override common law evidence principles or personal knowledge requirements; structurally, the argument is partially valid but overstated
stress_test.step_3_adversarial_challenge: Counter-argument: personal knowledge requirement is not from CrPC but from general evidence law (hearsay rule) and the Janki Vashdeo Bhojwani principle; Section 142's non-obstante clause covers CrPC specifically, not all other legal principles; further, allowing deposition without personal knowledge would undermine the accused's right to effective cross-examination

silent_arguments.the_argument: The non-obstante clause in Section 142 NI Act ('Notwithstanding anything contained in the Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973') excludes the application of the CPC's Order 3 Rules 1 and 2 framework and the Janki Vashdeo Bhojwani personal knowledge rule altogether in Section 138 proceedings — the NI Act's special regime should be entirely self-contained
silent_arguments.why_it_matters: If accepted, it would eliminate the personal knowledge qualification and explicit assertion requirement, simplifying the process for corporate and commercial complainants
silent_arguments.provision_or_principle: Section 142 NI Act (non-obstante clause); general principle of special law overriding general law (generalia specialibus non derogant)
silent_arguments.who_should_have_made_it: Respondents/complainants — would have supported a more complete victory (PoA holder with or without specific personal knowledge)

stress_test.step_1_statutory_basis: Section 200 CrPC provides for examination; para 22 of the judgment makes this discretionary, not mandatory; this argument would require the Court to read the discretion as mandatory in personal knowledge cases — a contradiction of RATIO_4
stress_test.step_2_structural_validity: Gate 3 analysis: Section 200 examination and the personal knowledge requirement operate at different stages; Section 200 is pre-cognizance procedural; personal knowledge is a substantive qualification for deposition; these are structurally distinct and the argument conflates them
stress_test.step_3_adversarial_challenge: Counter: para 26(iv) expressly says Magistrate is 'not mandatorily obliged'; forcing mandatory examination would contradict the three-judge bench ratio; the complaint's explicit assertion is the chosen mechanism; the accused can challenge knowledge at trial stage

silent_arguments.the_argument: The Magistrate's power to examine the complainant under Section 200 CrPC should apply specifically to verify the PoA holder's claimed personal knowledge of the transaction before issuing process, as a special gatekeeping measure — the assertion in the complaint is insufficient without judicial verification at the pre-cognizance stage
silent_arguments.why_it_matters: If the Magistrate must verify the PoA holder's personal knowledge before issuing process, many complaints filed with formulaic assertions would fail at the threshold
silent_arguments.provision_or_principle: Section 200 CrPC (examination of complainant), Section 202 CrPC [NOT EXTRACTABLE — INDEPENDENT VERIFICATION REQUIRED as to whether Section 202 inquiry was relevant]; the personal knowledge requirement from RATIO_2/RATIO_3
silent_arguments.who_should_have_made_it: Appellant/accused — would have strengthened the challenge to process issuance

stress_test.step_1_statutory_basis: No specific NI Act provision differentiates between individual and corporate complainants; Section 142(a) treats both as 'payee or holder in due course' without distinction
stress_test.step_2_structural_validity: Gate 3: The argument would require reading a corporate exception into RATIO_3's explicit assertion requirement; the Court in para 26(iii) makes no such distinction and appears to apply the requirement uniformly
stress_test.step_3_adversarial_challenge: Counter: the explicit assertion requirement serves evidentiary integrity regardless of whether complainant is individual or company; a company can only 'know' through its officers, but the specific officer signing the complaint must have actual knowledge of the specific transactions — a blanket presumption would be too broad and open to abuse

silent_arguments.the_argument: A company complainant should be treated differently from an individual payee — for a company, any duly authorised officer or PoA holder necessarily represents the company's 'knowledge' since companies can only act through agents, and therefore the personal knowledge requirement should be presumed satisfied for corporate complainants
silent_arguments.why_it_matters: Corporate complainants form a large proportion of Section 138 cases; if corporate PoA holder's knowledge is presumed, the explicit assertion requirement in the complaint is effectively waived for companies
silent_arguments.provision_or_principle: Companies Act provisions on company representation [INDEPENDENT VERIFICATION REQUIRED for specific provision]; general principle that corporations act through agents
silent_arguments.who_should_have_made_it: Respondent in second appeal (M/s Surana Securities Ltd.) — would have simplified its position

stress_test.step_1_statutory_basis: Neither Section 142 NI Act nor Section 200/204 CrPC expressly requires production of the PoA instrument at the time of filing; the court in para 10 notes this argument but the judgment does not expressly rule on it as a mandatory requirement
stress_test.step_2_structural_validity: Gate 3: Section 204 process issuance is at Magistrate's discretion after considering the complaint and documents; the PoA instrument is a document supporting the complaint but its absence may be a curable defect rather than a jurisdictional bar
stress_test.step_3_adversarial_challenge: Counter: subsequent production of PoA (as happened in para 10 — produced with reply to discharge application) suggests it is not a mandatory pre-condition; the Court's para 26 answers do not make PoA production timing a mandatory requirement; treating it as mandatory would be adding a condition not found in the statute

silent_arguments.the_argument: The requirement that the Power of Attorney be produced at the time of filing the complaint (or at least before process is issued) should be treated as mandatory, not curable by subsequent production — the Magistrate should not issue process without verifying the PoA instrument
silent_arguments.why_it_matters: If production of PoA instrument is mandatory before process issuance, complaints without filed PoA instruments would be non-maintainable ab initio and process would be illegal
silent_arguments.provision_or_principle: Section 200 CrPC read with Section 204 CrPC; Section 142(a) NI Act eligibility requirement
silent_arguments.who_should_have_made_it: Appellant A.C. Narayanan — this specific point was raised (para 10) that PoA was not filed with complaint or verifying statement

hidden_assumptions.self_check: This assumption has been accepted by a three-judge bench and is now authoritative; challenge would require a larger bench; practically difficult but theoretically available
hidden_assumptions.why_it_matters: Section 142 is a criminal statute with a non-obstante clause; criminal statutes are generally construed strictly; there is no express provision in the NI Act or CrPC incorporating agency law into the definition of 'complaint made by the payee'
hidden_assumptions.how_to_challenge: Argue that criminal law requires a strict reading; the non-obstante clause in Section 142 excludes CPC concepts; the Legislature could have expressly provided for PoA filing as it did in other statutes but did not; agency law is a civil construct and should not be read into criminal eligibility conditions
hidden_assumptions.where_it_operates: Para 21 — the entire reasoning supporting SYL_1 and RATIO_1 rests on transplanting civil agency law into the criminal domain of Section 142 NI Act
hidden_assumptions.what_court_assumes: That the general law of agency (acts of agent within authority = acts of principal) applies seamlessly to criminal proceedings under a special statute

hidden_assumptions.self_check: The synthesis is creative but functional; the real conflict may re-emerge in cases where the PoA holder has partial knowledge
hidden_assumptions.why_it_matters: The Division Bench that made the reference explicitly found conflict warranting larger bench consideration; characterising these as non-conflicting may obscure the genuine tension between the two
hidden_assumptions.how_to_challenge: Argue that Janki Vashdeo Bhojwani's holding that PoA holder 'cannot depose for the principal for the acts done by the principal and not by him' is genuinely in conflict with MMTC's broader permission; the personal knowledge synthesis is the Court's own creation, not present in either precedent
hidden_assumptions.where_it_operates: Para 26 — the framing of the resolution
hidden_assumptions.what_court_assumes: That there is 'no serious conflict' between MMTC and Janki Vashdeo Bhojwani (para 26)

hidden_assumptions.self_check: Court's answer in para 22 shows that Magistrate has discretion to examine; this partially addresses the concern but the assumption remains that assertion alone is ordinarily sufficient
hidden_assumptions.why_it_matters: If the explicit assertion is false or exaggerated, the safeguard is meaningless at the process issuance stage; the accused has no opportunity to challenge the assertion before process is issued
hidden_assumptions.how_to_challenge: Argue that a mere pleading assertion of knowledge is insufficient; Magistrate should at minimum verify or examine the PoA holder on this specific aspect before issuing process
hidden_assumptions.where_it_operates: Paras 23, 26(iii) — the requirement of explicit assertion in the complaint is treated as a sufficient safeguard
hidden_assumptions.what_court_assumes: That 'due knowledge' can be sufficiently established by explicit assertion in the complaint without requiring the Magistrate to independently verify that knowledge at the pre-cognizance stage

hidden_assumptions.self_check: [PROVISION TEXT OF SECTION 145 NOT REPRODUCED IN JUDGMENT — INDEPENDENT VERIFICATION REQUIRED to assess whether Section 145 explicitly covers the Section 200 stage or is limited to trial stage evidence]
hidden_assumptions.why_it_matters: Section 200 CrPC uses 'shall' (mandatory language); Section 145 NI Act enables affidavit evidence but does not on its face say examination under Section 200 is dispensed with; the Court reads this discretionary effect into Section 145 through the non-obstante clause in Section 142
hidden_assumptions.how_to_challenge: Argue that Section 145 speaks to evidence at trial/hearing stage, not to the pre-cognizance examination under Section 200; the non-obstante clause in Section 142 applies to cognizance conditions, not to the manner of examination; the mandatory language of Section 200 ('shall') should prevail
hidden_assumptions.what_court_assumes: That Section 145 NI Act's affidavit mechanism effectively displaces the 'shall examine' language of Section 200 CrPC for Section 138 complaints

hidden_assumptions.self_check: The term 'due knowledge' in para 26(ii) is deliberately broad and leaves this question open for future determination

genome_summary.text: A.C. Narayanan is the authoritative three-judge bench pronouncement on the Power of Attorney holder's role in Section 138 NI Act proceedings. It synthesises the apparent conflict between MMTC (liberal filing by PoA holder) and Janki Vashdeo Bhojwani (personal knowledge requirement for deposition) through harmonious construction and agency law. The judgment establishes a four-point framework: (1) PoA filing in the payee's name is valid under Section 142(a); (2) PoA holder may depose only with personal knowledge of the transaction; (3) such knowledge must be explicitly asserted in the complaint; (4) Section 145 NI Act makes Magistrate's Section 200 CrPC examination discretionary. A fifth rule on sub-delegation (explicit authorisation required) addresses corporate chain-of-authority issues. The judgment's primary vulnerability is the undefined 'due knowledge' standard and the explicit assertion requirement — both generate continuing fact-specific litigation without undermining the core holding.

items.label: PoA filing satisfies Section 142(a) eligibility
items.conclusion: The eligibility criterion of Section 142(a) is satisfied; the complaint is maintainable
items.major_premise: Section 142(a) NI Act requires a complaint 'made by the payee or the holder in due course'; when a PoA holder files in the name and on behalf of the payee, the complaint is the payee's complaint through an agent
items.minor_premise: In the present cases, the complaints were filed in the name of and on behalf of the respective payees through their constituted attorney/PoA holder; the PoA holder was not filing in his own name

items.label: Agency law principle supports PoA holder acting as complainant
items.conclusion: The initiation of proceedings is by the grantor represented by the attorney holder, not by the attorney holder in personal capacity; this is consistent with Section 142(a)
items.major_premise: The PoA holder is the agent of the grantor; acts done by the agent within authorised scope are acts of the principal (general agency law principle)
items.minor_premise: The grantor authorised the PoA holder to initiate legal proceedings; the PoA holder did so on behalf of and in the name of the payee

items.label: Deposition by PoA holder conditional on personal knowledge
items.conclusion: PoA holder may depose if he has personal knowledge of the transaction; this must be explicitly asserted in the complaint; if no such knowledge, PoA holder cannot be examined
items.major_premise: Janki Vashdeo Bhojwani holds that a PoA holder can only depose on facts within his personal knowledge; he cannot depose for acts done by the principal alone; however, if the PoA holder was personally involved/aware of the transaction as agent, he can depose
items.minor_premise: In NI Act Section 138 proceedings, the deponent must have personal knowledge of the transaction; where the PoA holder is in charge of business and alone is aware of transactions, he has that personal knowledge

items.label: Section 145 NI Act renders Section 200 CrPC examination discretionary
items.conclusion: In Section 138 complaints, the Magistrate may rely on the affidavit verification and is not mandatorily bound to examine the complainant under Section 200 CrPC; the examination remains discretionary
items.major_premise: Section 145 NI Act (inserted by Act 55 of 2002) enables evidence by affidavit in Section 138 proceedings; Section 200 CrPC examination is a general provision
items.minor_premise: Section 142 NI Act has a non-obstante clause overriding CrPC; Section 145 specifically provides for affidavit-based verification; the special statute's mechanism applies

items.label: Sub-delegation requires explicit authorisation
items.conclusion: Sub-delegation without explicit authorisation in the PoA instrument is invalid; however, the original PoA can be cancelled and a fresh one granted to another
items.major_premise: An agent cannot sub-delegate authority unless the principal has expressly authorised sub-delegation (delegatus non potest delegare as modified by express authority)
items.minor_premise: The general power of attorney's terms govern the scope of the PoA holder's authority; without an explicit sub-delegation clause, the PoA holder has no power to appoint a further agent

interpretive_method.method_basis: Court harmoniously constructed Sections 138, 142 and 145 of the NI Act with Section 200 CrPC, and read MMTC and Janki Vashdeo Bhojwani together without holding them in direct conflict
interpretive_method.judges_tendency: Pragmatic harmonious construction with sensitivity to commercial reality; reluctance to create procedural barriers that would frustrate NI Act's remedial purpose
interpretive_method.method_explanation: The Court adopted a harmonious construction approach: it held that there is no 'serious conflict' between MMTC and Janki Vashdeo Bhojwani (para 26) by reading the personal knowledge qualification from Janki Vashdeo Bhojwani into the MMTC principle of filing competence. It further harmonised Section 200 CrPC (general examination requirement) with Section 145 NI Act (special affidavit provision) by holding that the special provision prevails through Section 142's non-obstante clause, making examination discretionary. The Court also employed purposive interpretation in para 19 by reference to the practical need to accommodate inability of payees to appear due to sickness, old age, death or distance.

analysis_steps: Step 1: Section 142(a) uses 'payee or holder in due course' — PoA holder is neither
Step 2: Agency law principle that agent's acts = principal's acts is NOT on the face of the statute
Step 3: This reading would render MMTC wrong and would prevent most corporate complaints from being filed
alternative_method_analysis.analysis_narrative: A purely literal reading of Section 142(a) — 'complaint made by the payee or holder in due course' — could exclude PoA holder filing entirely, as the PoA holder is neither the payee nor the holder in due course. Similarly, a literal reading of Section 200 CrPC requires examination of 'the complainant' which could be read as excluding affidavit substitution.
alternative_method_analysis.likely_alternative_outcome: Complaints filed by PoA holders would be held non-maintainable; significant disruption to commercial practice

analysis_steps: Step 1: Purpose of Section 138 is to deter cheque dishonour and provide effective remedy to payees
Step 2: Personal knowledge qualification may frustrate purpose where payee is genuinely unable to appear
Step 3: However, such approach would remove cross-examination protections for accused
alternative_method_analysis.analysis_narrative: A purely purposive reading focused on the mischief of cheque dishonour and the legislative intent to provide a quick remedy for payees could have gone further and dispensed with the personal knowledge requirement entirely, allowing any duly authorised agent to depose.
alternative_method_analysis.likely_alternative_outcome: Outcome similar but personal knowledge requirement might have been softened further

rhetorical_architecture.tone: Authoritative but conciliatory; avoids declaring either precedent wrong; emphasises practical necessity (sickness, old age, death, overseas residence at para 19)
rhetorical_architecture.opening_move: Court begins by extracting the reference order verbatim (para 7), signalling deference to the prior Division Bench's identification of conflict, thereby legitimising the three-judge bench exercise
rhetorical_architecture.metaphor_or_analogy: Agency law as the conceptual bridge — the Court uses the master-agent/principal-agent relationship as the organising metaphor throughout paras 21-24, treating the PoA holder's acts as the grantor's acts
rhetorical_architecture.strategic_ambiguity: The phrase 'witnessed the transaction as an agent of the payee/holder in due course or possess due knowledge regarding the said transactions' (para 26(ii)) — 'due knowledge' is undefined and leaves room for future litigation on the threshold of knowledge required
rhetorical_architecture.persuasion_technique: Resolution by synthesis — the Court characterises MMTC and Janki Vashdeo Bhojwani as not in 'serious conflict' (para 26) and synthesises them: MMTC provides the rule (PoA can file), Janki Vashdeo Bhojwani provides the qualification (personal knowledge required for deposition). This avoids overruling either precedent and presents the judgment as clarification, not correction.

sword_uses.scenario: Defending maintainability of a Section 138 complaint filed by a PoA holder on behalf of an individual or company payee
sword_uses.how_to_cite: A.C. Narayanan vs State of Maharashtra, (2014) 11 SCC 790, para 26(i) — three-judge bench holding that filing of complaint petition under Section 138 NI Act through power of attorney is 'perfectly legal and competent'
sword_uses.draft_argument: The complaint in question is filed in the name of the payee/complainant and signed by the constituted attorney holding a duly executed Power of Attorney. This Court's three-judge bench in A.C. Narayanan (2014) 11 SCC 790 has authoritatively held (para 26(i)) that filing of a complaint petition under Section 138 of the NI Act through a Power of Attorney is perfectly legal and competent. The eligibility criterion of Section 142(a) is satisfied when the complaint is filed in the name of and on behalf of the payee or holder in due course. The present complaint meets this standard.
sword_uses.stage_of_proceedings: At process issuance stage; in response to quashing petition/discharge application

sword_uses.scenario: Resisting a discharge application or Section 482 CrPC quashing petition challenging the PoA holder's verification/affidavit in lieu of Section 200 CrPC examination
sword_uses.how_to_cite: A.C. Narayanan vs State of Maharashtra, (2014) 11 SCC 790, para 26(iv) — Magistrate is not mandatorily obliged to examine complainant and can rely on affidavit verification under Section 145 NI Act
sword_uses.draft_argument: The Magistrate's reliance on the affidavit verification filed by the complainant in support of the complaint under Section 138 NI Act is fully justified under this Court's three-judge bench decision in A.C. Narayanan (2014) 11 SCC 790 (para 26(iv)). The Magistrate is neither mandatorily obliged to call upon the complainant to remain present before Court nor to examine the complainant under oath before deciding to issue process. The affidavit filed in support of the complaint is sufficient compliance with the procedure for issuance of process under Section 200 CrPC read with Section 145 NI Act.
sword_uses.stage_of_proceedings: In High Court Section 482 CrPC petitions; in discharge applications before trial court

sword_uses.scenario: Challenging a sub-delegated PoA's authority where original PoA holder has appointed a further agent without express authorisation in the PoA instrument
sword_uses.how_to_cite: A.C. Narayanan vs State of Maharashtra, (2014) 11 SCC 790, para 26(v) — sub-delegation requires explicit clause in general power of attorney
sword_uses.draft_argument: The complaint in the present case was filed by a person purporting to act under a power of attorney granted not by the payee/complainant directly, but by a sub-delegate of the original PoA holder. The three-judge bench in A.C. Narayanan (2014) 11 SCC 790 has specifically held (para 26(v)) that the authority to sub-delegate functions under a general power of attorney must be explicitly mentioned in the general power of attorney itself. In the absence of such explicit sub-delegation clause, the sub-delegation is inconsistent with the general power of attorney and invalid in law. The complaint filed by the unauthorised sub-delegate is therefore not maintainable under Section 142(a) NI Act.
sword_uses.stage_of_proceedings: At process issuance stage; in discharge applications; in quashing petitions

sword_uses.scenario: Challenging the deposition of a PoA holder who lacks personal knowledge of the transaction — attacking the evidentiary basis of the complainant's case at trial
sword_uses.how_to_cite: A.C. Narayanan vs State of Maharashtra, (2014) 11 SCC 790, para 26(ii) and 26(iii) — PoA holder must have personal knowledge; no such knowledge = cannot be examined as witness
sword_uses.draft_argument: The Power of Attorney holder who has deposed in support of the present complaint has not demonstrated personal knowledge of the transactions underlying the cheque dishonour. The complaint itself contains no explicit assertion as to the PoA holder's knowledge of the specific transactions, as mandated by this Court's three-judge bench in A.C. Narayanan (2014) 11 SCC 790 (para 26(iii)). Further, the PoA holder was not personally involved in the transactions and derives his purported knowledge solely from company records — this does not satisfy the personal knowledge requirement (para 26(ii)). The examination of such a PoA holder without personal knowledge is impermissible and the deposition cannot be relied upon.
sword_uses.stage_of_proceedings: At trial stage during examination of complainant's witness; in appellate proceedings challenging conviction

shield_uses.attack_being_faced: Accused argues that complaint by PoA holder is not maintainable as PoA holder is not the payee or holder in due course under Section 142(a) NI Act
shield_uses.boundary_condition: This shield fails if the complaint was filed in the PoA holder's own name as complainant rather than in the payee's name (CF_1; para 21 — PoA holder 'cannot file a complaint in his own name as if he was the complainant')
shield_uses.how_judgment_helps: Para 26(i) — three-judge bench authoritatively holds that filing through PoA is perfectly legal and competent; RATIO_1 provides complete answer
shield_uses.key_factual_similarity: Complaint must be filed in the name of/on behalf of the payee, not in the personal name of the PoA holder

shield_uses.attack_being_faced: Accused argues that the Magistrate was mandatorily required to examine the complainant under Section 200 CrPC before issuing process and failure to do so vitiates the process
shield_uses.boundary_condition: This shield assumes Section 145 NI Act (inserted by Act 55 of 2002) applies; for complaints filed and processed before the 2002 amendment, this shield may not apply [INDEPENDENT VERIFICATION REQUIRED on the temporal scope of Section 145]
shield_uses.how_judgment_helps: Para 26(iv) — examination under Section 200 CrPC is discretionary, not mandatory, in Section 138 cases in light of Section 145 NI Act; Magistrate can rely on affidavit verification
shield_uses.key_factual_similarity: An affidavit in support of the complaint under Section 138 was filed by the complainant/PoA holder before process was issued

shield_uses.attack_being_faced: Accused argues that the PoA holder's testimony is inadmissible hearsay as he has no personal knowledge of the transactions
shield_uses.boundary_condition: The complaint must explicitly assert the PoA holder's knowledge (para 26(iii)); shield fails if explicit assertion is absent from the complaint or if PoA holder's only knowledge is derived from documents/third parties
shield_uses.how_judgment_helps: Paras 23 and 26(ii) — where the PoA holder is in charge of the business of the complainant-payee and the attorney holder alone is personally aware of the transactions, the PoA holder can depose as a witness
shield_uses.key_factual_similarity: PoA holder must be in charge of business and must have direct personal knowledge of the specific transactions

vulnerabilities.weak_point: The definition of 'due knowledge' in para 26(ii) is undefined — the Court does not distinguish between direct personal knowledge, knowledge through documents, and knowledge as business manager
vulnerabilities.stress_test: Step 1: The Court uses 'due knowledge' without defining it — textual gap exists; Step 2: Janki Vashdeo Bhojwani requires 'personal knowledge' which is a higher standard than 'knowledge through records'; Step 3: Counter — commercial/corporate practice requires that business managers be treated as having sufficient knowledge of business transactions even if derived from records; verdict: INCLUDE_WITH_CAVEAT
vulnerabilities.attack_vector: In appeals against conviction, argue that the PoA holder's 'knowledge' was derived solely from company records, not personal participation in the transaction; this is insufficient under Janki Vashdeo Bhojwani's personal knowledge standard which the Court incorporated
vulnerabilities.likelihood_of_success: MEDIUM — Courts may accept this argument in cases where PoA holder clearly was not personally involved; however, in business contexts, knowledge through records may be treated as sufficient

vulnerabilities.weak_point: The requirement of 'explicit assertion as to knowledge' in the complaint (para 26(iii)) creates vulnerability where complainants use standard/template complaints without specific assertions
vulnerabilities.stress_test: Step 1: Para 26(iii) explicitly states requirement for specific assertion; Step 2: This is a mandatory substantive requirement, not a curable defect; Step 3: Counter — courts may treat deficiency as curable by amendment; or may infer knowledge from overall context of complaint; verdict: INCLUDE
vulnerabilities.attack_vector: Challenge the complaint at the pre-cognizance or discharge stage for failure to explicitly assert the PoA holder's personal knowledge of the specific transaction; if no such assertion is found, argue that the PoA holder cannot be examined and the complaint cannot proceed
vulnerabilities.likelihood_of_success: HIGH — This is a specific, verifiable requirement; many standard complaints may lack such explicit assertion

vulnerabilities.weak_point: The Court's holding that Section 200 CrPC examination is discretionary rests on an inference from Section 145 NI Act whose text is not reproduced in the judgment
vulnerabilities.stress_test: Step 1: Statutory text of Section 145 not in judgment — independent verification needed; Step 2: Even if Section 145 is confined to trial stage, Section 142's non-obstante clause provides alternative basis; Step 3: Counter — three-judge bench decision is binding; only Constitution Bench can overrule; verdict: INCLUDE_WITH_CAVEAT
vulnerabilities.attack_vector: Challenge the discretionary reading by arguing that Section 145 NI Act (as it actually reads) applies to a different stage of proceedings and does not expressly displace the mandatory language of Section 200 CrPC; argue that the mandatory examination is required
vulnerabilities.likelihood_of_success: LOW — Three-judge bench has authoritatively decided this; would require larger bench or legislative change

vulnerabilities.weak_point: The sub-delegation ruling (RATIO_5) creates uncertainty about the validity of complaints in corporate chain-of-authority situations where PoA holders routinely authorise subordinates informally
vulnerabilities.stress_test: Step 1: Para 26(v) clearly states sub-delegation without explicit clause is invalid; Step 2: The structural rule is clear; Step 3: Counter — the distinction between 'cancellation and fresh grant' vs 'sub-delegation' may be blurred in practice; courts may read explicit sub-delegation clauses broadly; verdict: INCLUDE
vulnerabilities.attack_vector: Where a company's authorised PoA holder has effectively sub-delegated complaint filing to a subordinate/clerk without an explicit sub-delegation clause, challenge the validity of the complaint as filed by an unauthorised person
vulnerabilities.likelihood_of_success: MEDIUM — Depends heavily on the specific facts and the terms of the original PoA instrument

vulnerability_map.durability_reasoning: The judgment is from a three-judge bench of the Supreme Court resolving a reference — it has high precedential authority and cannot be challenged except before a larger bench or by legislative amendment. The ratio on filing competence (RATIO_1) is highly durable. The vulnerabilities are in the undefined 'due knowledge' standard (VULN_1) and the explicit assertion requirement (VULN_2), which will generate fact-specific litigation but will not undermine the core ratio. The Section 145/Section 200 CrPC interface (VULN_3) is the most theoretically vulnerable but practically unlikely to succeed given the bench strength.

temporal_vulnerability.can_be_overruled_by: A Constitution Bench (5 or more judges) of the Supreme Court, or a larger bench specifically constituted to reconsider

No indication of pending legislative change as of extraction date. [INDEPENDENT VERIFICATION REQUIRED for any post-2013 NI Act amendments]

distinguishing_playbook.draft_argument: This Court in A.C. Narayanan (2014) 11 SCC 790 at para 21 has expressly held that 'the power of attorney holder cannot file a complaint in his own name as if he was the complainant.' The present complaint names the PoA holder as the complainant without identifying the payee as the principal. This is fundamentally different from the situation in A.C. Narayanan where the complaints were filed in the name of the respective payees through their PoA holder. The ratio of A.C. Narayanan does not apply and the complaint is not maintainable under Section 142(a).
distinguishing_playbook.factual_difference: The PoA holder filed the complaint in his own name as if he were the complainant, rather than in the name of the payee/principal

distinguishing_playbook.draft_argument: The ratio in A.C. Narayanan (2014) 11 SCC 790 at para 26(ii) specifically requires that the PoA holder must have 'witnessed the transaction as an agent of the payee/holder in due course or possess due knowledge regarding the said transactions.' In the present case, the PoA holder was appointed [after the transactions were complete / has no business connection to the underlying transaction]. Further, the complaint does not explicitly assert the PoA holder's knowledge as required by para 26(iii). These distinguishing facts take the present case outside the protective ratio of A.C. Narayanan.
distinguishing_playbook.factual_difference: The PoA holder has no personal knowledge of the transactions whatsoever — was appointed as a nominee after the transaction was complete and has no involvement in the business

distinguishing_playbook.draft_argument: A.C. Narayanan (2014) 11 SCC 790 at para 26(v) draws a critical distinction between a PoA holder (who can file complaints) and a sub-delegate appointed by the PoA holder without explicit authority. In the present case, [X] who filed the complaint was not appointed by the payee/complainant directly but was purportedly appointed by [Y], the original PoA holder, without any sub-delegation clause in the general power of attorney. The Court in A.C. Narayanan has held such sub-delegation to be invalid. The complaint is therefore filed by an unauthorised person and is not maintainable.
distinguishing_playbook.factual_difference: The person who filed/verified the complaint was not a PoA holder at all but a sub-delegate appointed by the PoA holder without an express sub-delegation clause

distinguishing_playbook.draft_argument: Para 26(iii) of A.C. Narayanan (2014) 11 SCC 790 requires that 'it is required by the complainant to make specific assertion as to the knowledge of the power of attorney holder in the said transaction explicitly in the complaint.' The present complaint contains no such explicit assertion. This is a specific mandatory requirement laid down by the three-judge bench; its absence distinguishes this case and renders the deposition of the PoA holder impermissible, undermining the evidentiary foundation of the complaint.
distinguishing_playbook.factual_difference: The complaint does not contain any explicit assertion as to the PoA holder's personal knowledge of the transaction

cross_domain_transplants.viability: MEDIUM — the principle is analogous but applicability depends on the specific wording of each statute's eligibility/cognizance provision
cross_domain_transplants.target_domain: Other special statutes requiring complaint by a specific person — e.g., Prevention of Food Adulteration Act, Drug and Cosmetics Act complaints by authorised persons
cross_domain_transplants.original_domain: Negotiable Instruments Act — Section 138 cheque dishonour complaints

gate_7_validation.step_a_target_provision: [SPECIFIC STATUTORY PROVISION IN TARGET DOMAIN REQUIRES INDEPENDENT VERIFICATION — each special statute has its own eligibility condition; cannot generalise without examining the exact text]
gate_7_validation.step_b_contextual_analogy: The principle that agency law applies to satisfy personal complainant requirements is potentially transferable where: (i) the statute requires complaint by a specific person; (ii) there is no express exclusion of PoA holders; (iii) the statute's purpose is not defeated by PoA filing
gate_7_validation.step_c_specialized_override: Many special statutes (e.g., Prevention of Money Laundering Act, certain SEBI regulations) have specific authorisation provisions that may override or supplement the general A.C. Narayanan principle; domain-specific framework may be complete in itself

cross_domain_transplants.analogous_principle: Where a special statute requires a complaint to be filed by a specific person/category, a duly authorised PoA holder or agent acting in the name and on behalf of that specific person satisfies the eligibility criterion; the personal knowledge requirement for deposition applies

cross_domain_transplants.viability: HIGH — Janki Vashdeo Bhojwani itself was a civil case; A.C. Narayanan endorsed the Janki Vashdeo Bhojwani personal knowledge principle; the civil domain is the original source of this principle
cross_domain_transplants.target_domain: Civil proceedings — PoA holder as plaintiff or as witness in title suits, recovery suits
cross_domain_transplants.original_domain: Criminal proceedings under NI Act — PoA holder deposition

gate_7_validation.step_a_target_provision: Order 3 Rules 1 and 2 CPC — specifically mentioned in both Janki Vashdeo Bhojwani and A.C. Narayanan
gate_7_validation.step_b_contextual_analogy: Direct — A.C. Narayanan transplants the CPC personal knowledge principle into the NI Act criminal domain; using it in reverse (back to civil domain) is even more direct
gate_7_validation.step_c_specialized_override: CPC itself governs civil proceedings; no specialized override; Janki Vashdeo Bhojwani is the primary authority in civil domain and A.C. Narayanan reinforces it

cross_domain_transplants.analogous_principle: A PoA holder with personal knowledge of the transaction can depose in civil proceedings for and on behalf of the principal in respect of acts done within the scope of the PoA; cannot depose on matters of which only the principal has personal knowledge

cross_domain_transplants.viability: MEDIUM — the delegatus non potest delegare principle is well-established across domains; A.C. Narayanan applies it specifically in the PoA context; applicability to board resolutions depends on corporate law framework
cross_domain_transplants.target_domain: Corporate law — board resolutions authorising sub-committees or officers to file complaints/initiate proceedings under other statutes (Companies Act, SEBI Act, etc.)

gate_7_validation.step_a_target_provision: [SPECIFIC PROVISION IN COMPANIES ACT/SEBI ACT GOVERNING DELEGATION REQUIRES INDEPENDENT VERIFICATION — likely Sections 179, 180 Companies Act 2013 or relevant SEBI Regulations but cannot confirm without independent verification]
gate_7_validation.step_b_contextual_analogy: The principle is analogous but corporate law has its own scheme of delegation (board resolution, committee delegation) which may be governed by specific statutory provisions that define the scope of sub-delegation differently
gate_7_validation.step_c_specialized_override: Companies Act 2013 has specific provisions on delegation of powers; these may constitute a specialized framework that partially overrides or supplements the general A.C. Narayanan principle

cross_domain_transplants.analogous_principle: Sub-delegation of authority requires explicit authorisation in the original instrument of delegation; without such express provision, sub-delegation is invalid; however, the principal can cancel the original delegation and grant a fresh one to another person
